decisions.title: ARCH-1: Fail-open — dispatch always exits 0 on error
decisions.rationale: Hookwise runs inside Claude Code's hook lifecycle, where a non-zero exit from the dispatcher would surface as a tool error to the user. To guarantee hook infrastructure never disrupts the host session, every error path — malformed config, dispatch timeout, panic, notification failure — is logged but exits 0. A blocked tool still returns exit 0; the block is communicated via JSON stdout, not the exit code. Sources: internal/core/dispatcher.go:20 ("Follows fail-open guarantee (ARCH-1)."); internal/core/errors.go:126; cmd/hookwise/cmd_dispatch.go:51-52; internal/core/guards.go:179; internal/integration/integration_test.go:210 ("ARCH-1: blocked tool still returns exit 0").
decisions.consequences: Hookwise can never break the user's session, but config parse errors and side-effect failures are silent (no output, exit 0) — bad config looks identical to no config. Errors are observable only via the warning log.

decisions.title: ARCH-2: Single-writer SQLite connection (SetMaxOpenConns(1)) under WAL
decisions.rationale: The analytics DB opens in WAL mode (journal_mode=WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, busy_timeout=5000) and pins the pool to one writer via SetMaxOpenConns(1). WAL permits concurrent readers plus one writer, so the single-writer cap is now a deliberate write-safety choice rather than the Dolt single-threading workaround it originally was. Consequently CommitDispatch is a no-op: WAL makes writes immediately visible across connections. Sources: internal/analytics/sqlite.go:86-89 ("ARCH-2: keep a single writer connection ... NOT a backend limitation as it was under Dolt embedded."); internal/analytics/sqlite.go:76-79 (WAL DSN pragmas); cmd/hookwise/cmd_dispatch.go:230-231 (CommitDispatch no-op). See also [[single-writer-wal-rationale]].
decisions.consequences: Writes are serialized through one connection (no write contention, no lock errors) while reads still scale; no explicit commit/transaction-boundary code is needed. The cap is rationale-driven, not backend-forced.

decisions.title: ARCH-3: Daemon writes JSON cache only, never the analytics DB
decisions.rationale: The feeds daemon polls producers and writes per-feed JSON cache files; it must never touch analytics.db. The split was originally motivated by Dolt's filesystem-lock behaviour and is retained for architectural clarity under WAL. It is enforced structurally: an architecture-lint test fails the build if internal/feeds/... imports internal/analytics, and feed-scheduling lives in cmd/hookwise (package main), not in the feeds package. Sources: internal/arch/arch_test.go:104-116 (TestArch_FeedsDoNotImportAnalytics); internal/feeds/polling.go:106 ("ARCH-3: Write to JSON cache only, NOT the analytics DB."); cmd/hookwise/cmd_daemon.go:151; internal/integration/integration_test.go:674-731 (Test 8: Daemon ARCH-3 compliance); docs/decisions/0001-dolt-to-sqlite.md:52.
decisions.consequences: Only the dispatch process writes analytics, preventing the daemon from contending for the single writer connection; the import boundary is machine-checked so the rule can't silently erode.

decisions.title: ARCH-5: First-match-wins guard evaluation
decisions.rationale: Guard rules are evaluated against a tool call as an ordered list with firewall-style first-match-wins semantics: rules are iterated in order, the first matching rule's action and reason are returned immediately, and no match yields allow. Guards are evaluated on PreToolUse events only. Ordering is load-bearing — a glob rule placed before an exact rule wins over it. Sources: internal/core/guards.go:171-179 ("First matching rule wins ... No match = allow"); internal/core/dispatcher.go:43 ("Evaluated on PreToolUse events only — first match wins"); internal/core/guards_test.go:524; testdata/contracts/12_pretooluse_first_match_wins.json, 13_pretooluse_first_match_exact_before_glob.json.
decisions.consequences: Rule order in config is significant and must be authored deliberately; later rules are unreachable once an earlier rule matches. The behaviour is pinned by contract fixtures so output stays byte-stable.

decisions.title: ARCH-6: Contract parity — byte-identical stdout per JSON fixtures
decisions.rationale: Hookwise's stdout JSON must be byte-identical to the original TypeScript implementation it was migrated from. This is enforced by contract tests that drive fixtures in testdata/contracts/ and compare output byte-exactly. The suite mandates coverage minimums (all event types and guard operators, >=20 fixtures; 33 currently exist). Sources: internal/contract/contract_test.go:3-5 ("byte-identical stdout JSON between Go and TypeScript (ARCH-6)"); contract_test.go:183 ("Byte-exact comparison"); contract_test.go:345 (">=20 contract fixtures"); testdata/contracts/ (33 fixtures).
decisions.consequences: Any change to stdout formatting is a contract break caught at test time; cross-language output compatibility is guaranteed but output structure is effectively frozen unless fixtures are deliberately updated.

decisions.title: ARCH-7: Side effects non-blocking with per-goroutine recover()
decisions.rationale: Phase 3 of dispatch fires side-effect handlers as goroutines so they never block the hook response. Each goroutine carries its own defer/recover boundary, so a panic in one handler is logged and contained rather than crashing the process. The same pattern guards the daemon's socket-serving goroutine so the daemon stays alive across handler panics. Sources: internal/core/dispatcher.go:186-190 ("Each goroutine has its own defer/recover boundary (ARCH-7)"); dispatcher.go:213-221 (recover in side-effect goroutine); internal/feeds/socket.go:92; cmd/hookwise/cmd_daemon.go:189.
decisions.consequences: Side effects (analytics writes, notifications) run concurrently and a single panicking handler cannot take down dispatch or the daemon; the trade-off is that side-effect failures are observable only via logs, consistent with [[arch1-fail-open]].

decisions.title: Replace Dolt embedded backend with modernc.org/sqlite + periodic snapshots (PR #74)
decisions.rationale: The Dolt embedded driver carried a ~137-144 MB resident footprint per process; the test binary was ~149 MB (~300 MB under -race), which directly caused the retro-009 kernel panic and retro-010 chronic memory overhead. Dolt was replaced with the pure-Go, CGO-free modernc.org/sqlite driver (~19 MB stripped) in WAL mode, and Dolt's versioned-history features were reimplemented via VACUUM INTO snapshots so `hookwise log`/`hookwise diff` still work. Status: Accepted, 2026-06-07. Sources: docs/decisions/0001-dolt-to-sqlite.md; git commit 1218be9 ("Replace Dolt with modernc SQLite + periodic snapshots (#74)"); .claude/specs/retro/retro-009-system-crash-memory-exhaustion.md, retro-010-hook-memory-audit.md; internal/analytics/sqlite.go:5-6.
decisions.consequences: An order-of-magnitude smaller binary and memory footprint eliminated the memory-exhaustion failure mode; [[arch2-single-writer-sqlite]] and [[arch3-daemon-writes-cache-not-db]] lose their Dolt-specific motivations and are re-annotated as deliberate-by-rationale rather than backend-forced.

decisions.title: Single-writer-under-WAL is now a deliberate choice, not a Dolt requirement
decisions.rationale: SetMaxOpenConns(1) predates the SQLite migration: under Dolt embedded it was a forced single-threading workaround. Post-PR-#74, WAL mode allows concurrent readers plus exactly one writer, so the cap is retained as an intentional write-safety decision rather than a backend constraint. The downstream consequence is that CommitDispatch became a no-op because WAL makes writes immediately visible across connections. Sources: internal/analytics/sqlite.go:86-89; docs/decisions/0001-dolt-to-sqlite.md:51; cmd/hookwise/cmd_dispatch.go:230-231. Refines [[arch2-single-writer-sqlite]]; follows from [[dolt-to-sqlite-migration]].
decisions.consequences: The constraint is now rationale-driven and could in principle be relaxed; keeping it serializes writes safely with no explicit transaction boundaries, and it is documented as such so future maintainers don't mistake it for a hard backend limit.

decisions.title: Session cost computed on the Stop/SessionEnd seam; transcript .jsonl is the only token source (PRs #100, #101, #103)
decisions.rationale: Real per-session cost is computed only on the EventStop/EventSessionEnd seam, and only when cost is enabled and a transcript path is present — never on the per-tool PostToolUse hot path. transcript.SumUsage scans the session's .jsonl line-by-line (skipping non-assistant / no-usage lines) to produce per-model token usage, the single source of truth for tokens. pricing.ComputeWithRates then prices each model purely (built-in per-MTok rates for Opus/Sonnet/Haiku, case-insensitive substring match, unknown models falling back to Sonnet), and the accumulated total is persisted to cost_state. Sources: cmd/hookwise/cmd_dispatch.go:197-217; internal/transcript/transcript.go (SumUsage); internal/pricing/pricing.go (ComputeWithRates); git commits dcdc6a8 (#100 pricing), b32d709 (#101 transcript reader), 4bb605e (#103 compute cost on Stop + write cost_state).
decisions.consequences: Cost is derived deterministically from the transcript rather than estimated mid-stream, so it is reproducible and isolated to a single seam off the hot path; models the pricing table doesn't recognize are silently priced as Sonnet, which can misprice novel models until rates are added.

decisions.title: Never auto-spawn the daemon from a go test (.test) binary (issue #84, PR #102)
decisions.rationale: Because the status-line command auto-starts the feeds daemon via spawnDaemon, and under `go test` os.Executable() resolves to the package's compiled <pkg>.test binary, re-exec'ing it as `<pkg>.test daemon run` would re-run the entire test suite — which calls back into spawnDaemon, forking an unbounded chain of test processes. That runaway is the mechanism behind the retro-009 kernel panic. spawnDaemon now refuses to launch when isTestBinary detects a `.test`/`.test.exe` suffix or when HOOKWISE_DISABLE_DAEMON_AUTOSTART=1, returning errDaemonAutostartDisabled and letting callers fail-open. Sources: internal/feeds/client.go:26-37 (isTestBinary), :21-24 (errDaemonAutostartDisabled), :125-128 (spawnDaemon guard); git commit 3b3d3fc ("never auto-spawn daemon from a go test binary (#84) (#102)"); internal/feeds/client_test.go:111-130.
decisions.consequences: Test runs can never fork-bomb the host via daemon auto-start, eliminating the retro-009 memory-exhaustion path; daemon auto-start is also scriptably disable-able via an env var for non-test contexts. Follows [[arch1-fail-open]].

decisions.title: The live ARCH constraint set is {ARCH-1,2,3,5,6,7}; ARCH-4 and ARCH-8 are retired
decisions.rationale: Although CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md reference "8 ARCH constraints," no ARCH-4 or ARCH-8 exists in any current production source file. They survive only in stale, pre-SQLite artifacts: ARCH-4 ("Native YAML tags") in per-spec validation-criteria docs that each redefined it locally, and ARCH-8 ("Batch Dolt commits") in a stale worktree copy of the removed Dolt backend. Both were Dolt-era concepts retired with PR #74. Sources: grep across internal/ and cmd/ finds no ARCH-4/ARCH-8 in production; .claude/specs/go-migration/validation-criteria.md (stale ARCH-4); .claude/worktrees/coderabbit-fix/internal/analytics/dolt.go (stale ARCH-8). Relates to [[dolt-to-sqlite-migration]].
decisions.consequences: ADRs and docs should treat the live constraint set as {ARCH-1, ARCH-2, ARCH-3, ARCH-5, ARCH-6, ARCH-7}; citing ARCH-4 or ARCH-8 as current would be fabrication. The "8 constraints" claim in CLAUDE.md/MEMORY.md is stale and should be corrected.

decisions.title: openlore Claude Code startup wiring installed with explicit user consent (.mcp.json + SessionStart orient hook)
decisions.rationale: openlore's Claude Code surface was wired via `openlore install --agent claude-code --no-analyze` after the operator gave explicit in-session consent on 2026-06-15. It writes three things: the managed OpenLore block in CLAUDE.md (tracked), .mcp.json registering the openlore MCP server `npx --yes openlore mcp` (tracked), and .claude/settings.json with a SessionStart hook running `npx --yes openlore orient --json` (gitignored, local-only). The install was scoped to the claude-code surface ONLY — the global Cursor surfaces (~/.cursorrules, ~/.cursor/*) were deliberately excluded. Earlier agent-initiated attempts (global npm install; install without consent) were correctly blocked by Claude Code's auto-mode safety classifier as self-modification of agent startup config on dangerous infrastructure; the wiring proceeded only once the operator authorized it.
decisions.consequences: Agents now auto-call orient() at session start in this repo, getting structural context before reading files. The MCP registration (.mcp.json) and CLAUDE.md managed block are git-tracked; the SessionStart hook (.claude/settings.json) stays local/gitignored. No global Cursor config was touched. To remove: `openlore install --agent claude-code --uninstall`.
